metric_value: IA servers gaining market share in depressed global market
notes: Enterprise buyers confident in reliability, performance, and value of Intel processors and chipsets

metric_value: Over 1 million enterprise chipsets shipped since beginning of 2002
notes: Primarily E7501, E7505, E7205 for Xeon and E8870 for Itanium 2

metric_value: Xeon with E7505 winning design and rendering benchmarks against RISC workstations
notes: AGP 8x support and over 4 GB/s memory bandwidth

metric_value: Over 4 GB/second
notes: E7505 chipset with Xeon processors for workstations

metric_value: 16 GB with 3 PCI-X buses and dual GbE
notes: Volume two-way server configuration for Xeon processors

metric_value: Nocona to debut in 2004 at higher clock speeds with PCI Express and DDR2 via Lindenhurst
notes: Lindenhurst expected to be industry's first chipset with PCI Express and DDR2

metric_value: Nocona launched June 2004 with EM64T, PCI Express, DDR2 support as predicted
notes: Prediction confirmed; also added 64-bit extensions not anticipated in white paper

metric_value: 4 to 32 processors, rivaling RISC servers at lower acquisition cost
notes: Used for enterprise applications, office automation, decision support

metric_value: Potomac with Twin Castle chipset expected by end of 2004
notes: Intel's first recent 4-way IA-32 enterprise chipset effort

metric_value: Winning numerous commercial and scientific benchmark tests
notes: Picking up market share against RISC/Unix and mainframe machines

metric_value: Itanium 2 positioned for large databases, decision support, and scientific/technical workloads
notes: High memory bandwidth and EPIC architecture cited as key advantages

metric_value: Itanium discontinued in 2021; never achieved mainstream adoption, lost to x86-64
notes: Paper's optimism about Itanium 2 proved incorrect; AMD64/EM64T extensions made 64-bit x86 dominant instead

metric_value: DDR266: 2.1 GB/s; DDR333: 2.6 GB/s; DDR400: 3.2 GB/s (6.4 GB/s dual-channel)
notes: DDR400 in two-channel configuration achieving 6.4 GB/s peak

metric_value: Server memory configurations quadrupled over past three years
notes: Greater capacity and lower prices driving growth; more chips as potential failure points

metric_value: DDR2-400 draws half the power of DDR400 and 40% less than DDR333
notes: Critical advantage for 1U rack-mounted servers and blades

metric_value: DDR2 to debut in 2004 as DDR2-400, enabling higher utilization and server consolidation
notes: Paper correctly predicted DDR2 would become dominant server memory standard

metric_value: DDR2 became mainstream server memory by 2005-2006; succeeded by DDR3 (2007), DDR4 (2014), DDR5 (2020)
notes: Prediction confirmed; DDR2 power advantages materialized as described

metric_value: Memory mirroring, spare memory, and memory scrubbing to migrate from Itanium 2 to mainstream servers
notes: Aberdeen expected mainframe-class memory RAS for volume servers in 2004

metric_value: Memory mirroring and scrubbing became standard in enterprise servers; ECC universal in server-grade memory
notes: Prediction confirmed; these features are now standard in all enterprise servers

metric_value: 875P supports ECC, SATA, dual-channel DDR400 with PAT, gigabit Ethernet
notes: Bridging gap between desktop and server-class technology at value prices

metric_value: About half of total data center TCO is labor costs for operations and administration
notes: Aberdeen research finding; drives focus on systems management automation

metric_value: Over 150 adopter companies; IPMI in use since 1998; v1.5 current
notes: Open hardware-management interface with standardized message-passing; should be checklist requirement

metric_value: IPMI to enable predictive failure alerts, self-healing, auto-provisioning, and improved asset tracking
notes: Paper predicted IPMI would become standard for heterogeneous server management

metric_value: IPMI became universal in servers; now being superseded by DMTF Redfish RESTful API standard
notes: IPMI v2.0 released 2004; Redfish 1.0 released 2015 as modern successor; IPMI's core concepts persist

metric_value: PCI: 532 MB/s at 66 MHz; PCI-X: 1.06 GB/s at 133 MHz; PCI-X 2.0: 2.1 GB/s at 266 MHz proposed
notes: PCI-X 533 MHz (4.2 GB/s) electrical specs not completed as of July 2003

metric_value: PCI Express layered architecture should lead to a decade or more of useful life
notes: Remarkably prescient prediction; PCIe has lasted over 20 years through PCIe 6.0 and counting
Paper's decade prediction proved conservative; architecture far exceeded expected lifespan

metric_value: Each PCI-E lane at 2.5 GHz (x1); up to x16 aggregation for 4 GB/s total
notes: Scalable lane aggregation was a key architectural innovation

metric_value: Serial graphics via PCI-E to replace AGP, delivering 4 GB/s (twice AGP 8x throughput)
notes: Paper correctly predicted PCI Express would supplant AGP for graphics

metric_value: AGP fully replaced by PCI Express for graphics by 2008; last AGP cards produced around 2007
notes: Prediction confirmed; PCI Express became universal for graphics cards

metric_value: Recommended transitioning directly to PCI Express rather than PCI-X 266 due to advantages and longevity
notes: Sound advice; PCI-X 266 had minimal adoption while PCI Express became dominant

metric_value: InfiniBand likely to blossom with PCI-E; native 4x (10 Gbps) no longer limited by PCI-X bus
notes: Expected server-to-server, server-to-storage, and TOE uses in 2004

metric_value: InfiniBand became dominant HPC/AI interconnect; NVIDIA acquired Mellanox for $7B in 2020
notes: Prediction partially confirmed; InfiniBand grew but primarily in HPC/AI, not general data center

metric_value: PCI Express x8 capable of meeting sustained bandwidth for 10GbE applications
notes: x4 InfiniBand and 10GbE to require four PCI-E data lanes for 10 Gbps

metric_value: Three critical technology building blocks: memory (DDR2), systems management (IPMI), and serial I/O (PCI Express)
notes: Framework for understanding 2004 server architecture transition

metric_value: No economic reason not to buy true server RAS for server roles; price gap decreased considerably
notes: Millions of servers worldwide are desktops cast in server roles; Canterwood-ES to bridge gap

metric_value: Data integrity checking, advanced error logging via IPMI, standards-based hot plug for mix-and-match peripherals
notes: Hot plugging enables mixing vendors' adapters in I/O card cages

metric_value: I/O consolidated and unified across buses; less duplication of electronics; lower server acquisition costs
notes: Computer utility concept easier and less costly in PCI-E environment

metric_value: Serial ATA supported by Canterwood 875P; serial disk connections to proliferate for SCSI and ATA drives
notes: Serial storage-in-a-box to support both SCSI and ATA in same enclosure

metric_value: Lindenhurst expected to be industry's first chipset to incorporate both PCI Express and DDR2
notes: Paired with Nocona processor for 2004 next-generation Xeon platform

metric_value: Extensive RAS logic for 2-8 way Itanium 2 servers; suited for mission-critical databases and decision support
notes: Larger systems designable using E8870 building blocks; some suppliers offering 2-way workstations

metric_value: PCI SIG developing enclosed Server I/O Module form factor for true plug and play with reduced handling damage
notes: Targeted for server and workstation applications

metric_value: New technology expected to cost less overall while providing better performance, higher RAS, and lower operations costs
notes: Aberdeen conclusion; described as 'a free lunch worth planning for'

metric_value: PCI Express is software compatible with PCI-X and PCI; no driver rewrites necessary
notes: Critical for enterprise adoption; backward software compatibility eased transition

metric_value: IPMI enables reduction in TCO through common software interfaces across multiple servers from multiple vendors
notes: Storage management and virtualization also supported; improves aggregate capacity utilization
